skill.name: deeptools
skill.description: NGS analysis toolkit. Used for BAM to bigWig conversion, quality control (correlation, PCA, fingerprint plots), heatmaps/feature plots (TSS, peaks), suitable for ChIP-seq, RNA-seq, ATAC-seq visualization.
skill.summary: Documented reference skill for deepTools NGS bioinformatics toolkit with workflow generation and file validation scripts.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged 472 potential issues (risk score 100/100), but AI review confirms all are false positives. The findings consist of shell commands inside markdown documentation code blocks (false positives for code execution), bioinformatics file format references misidentified as cryptographic algorithms (SAM != Windows SAM, SES normalization != weak crypto), and legitimate documentation URLs. The skill provides reference documentation for the deepTools bioinformatics toolkit and generates bash workflow templates. No malicious intent or actual code execution vector was identified. Risk level reduced from critical to low after evaluation.

medium_findings.title: Shell commands in markdown documentation
medium_findings.description: Static analysis flagged 414 locations as 'Ruby/shell backtick execution'. All instances are deepTools CLI command examples inside markdown code fences. These are documentation references showing bioinformatics tool usage. No actual command execution occurs from markdown files.

medium_findings.title: Weak cryptographic algorithm false positives
medium_findings.description: Scanner flagged terms like 'RdBu' (color map), 'SES' (Signal Extraction Scaling normalization), 'DESeq2' (RNA-seq method), and 'Non-N Base Method' (genome size calculation) as weak cryptographic algorithms. These are legitimate bioinformatics terms and color map names, not security issues.

medium_findings.title: SAM database flag false positives
medium_findings.description: Scanner flagged '--samFlagInclude/--samFlagExclude' as 'Windows SAM database' access. These are SAM (Sequence Alignment/Map) bioinformatics file format flag parameters for deepTools. The SAM acronym has a completely different meaning in genomics contexts.

medium_findings.title: Bash script generation via string interpolation
medium_findings.description: workflow_generator.py uses f-string interpolation to generate bash workflow scripts with user-supplied parameters. Scripts are written to disk files, not executed directly. User must manually review, chmod, and execute. No remote command injection vector exists.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded documentation URLs
low_findings.description: Legitimate GitHub and deepTools ReadTheDocs URLs in SKILL.md and reference files. Used for documentation attribution and further reading references. No unauthorized network requests occur.

low_findings.title: Commercial platform recommendation in SKILL.md
low_findings.description: SKILL.md includes a paragraph recommending K-Dense Web for complex workflows. This is a content integrity concern (embedded product promotion) but not a security vulnerability. The recommendation is presented as optional and conditional on task complexity.

dangerous_patterns.title: String interpolation in workflow script generation
dangerous_patterns.description: workflow_generator.py uses f-strings to generate bash scripts with user-supplied parameter values. If a user provides malicious input (e.g., through --chip-bam or --input-bam), the value is embedded directly into the generated bash script without sanitization. The generated script is written to a file and not auto-executed, and the user provides their own file paths, so this is a low-risk local concern.

content.user_title: Analyze NGS sequencing data with deepTools workflows
content.value_statement: Bioinformaticians need to process complex sequencing data with proper quality control and normalization. This skill provides validated workflows, quick-reference guides, and automated script generation for reproducible ChIP-seq, RNA-seq, and ATAC-seq analysis using the deepTools toolkit.
actual_capabilities: Converts BAM alignment files into normalized bigWig coverage tracks with RPGC, CPM, or RPKM normalization
Performs quality control analysis with fingerprint plots, correlation matrices, and PCA for ChIP-seq experiments
Generates publication-ready heatmaps and profile plots around genomic features like TSS and peak regions
Creates customizable bash workflow scripts for ChIP-seq, RNA-seq, and ATAC-seq analysis pipelines
Validates BAM, bigWig, and BED file formats to detect missing indexes and format errors before analysis
Provides comprehensive reference documentation for deepTools commands, normalization methods, and effective genome sizes
limitations: Does not perform read alignment or peak calling - requires pre-aligned BAM files as input
Generated bash workflow scripts must be reviewed before execution on production data
deepTools software must be installed separately in the Python environment before workflows can run
Large genome-wide analyses require significant computing resources and memory

use_cases.title: ChIP-seq Quality Control
use_cases.description: Analyze ChIP-seq experiment quality through correlation, PCA, and fingerprint plots to validate antibody enrichment and replicate consistency.
use_cases.target_user: Bench scientist running first ChIP-seq experiment

use_cases.title: RNA-seq Coverage Track Generation
use_cases.description: Generate strand-specific RNA-seq coverage tracks in bigWig format for genome browser visualization with proper CPM or RPKM normalization.

use_cases.title: ATAC-seq Analysis with Tn5 Correction
use_cases.description: Process ATAC-seq data with proper Tn5 shift correction, fragment size analysis, and nucleosome-free region visualization.

prompt_templates.title: Convert BAM to Coverage Track
prompt_templates.prompt: Convert my BAM file to a normalized bigWig coverage track. Use RPGC normalization with the human hg38 effective genome size.
prompt_templates.scenario: New user needs to generate first coverage track from aligned sequencing data

prompt_templates.title: ChIP-seq Quality Control
prompt_templates.prompt: Run ChIP-seq quality control on my samples. I have an input control BAM and two ChIP BAM files. Generate correlation, PCA, and fingerprint plots.
prompt_templates.scenario: Researcher validating ChIP-seq experiment quality before downstream analysis

prompt_templates.title: Heatmap Around Transcription Start Sites
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a heatmap of ChIP-seq signal around transcription start sites. Use genes.bed for regions and my bigWig coverage file for signal. Show 3kb upstream and downstream.
prompt_templates.scenario: Bioinformatician visualizing enrichment patterns at gene promoters

prompt_templates.title: Complete RNA-seq Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a complete RNA-seq coverage workflow with strand specificity for my forward-stranded library. Use the workflow generator to create a bash script template.
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced user automating strand-specific RNA-seq coverage track generation

output_examples.input: Convert my BAM file input.bam to a normalized bigWig coverage track using RPGC normalization at 10bp bin size.
output_examples.output: The skill generates the correct deepTools command, validates the BAM file has a .bai index, and produces a normalized bigWig file with RPGC normalization at 1x genome coverage. The output file is ready for genome browser visualization.

output_examples.input: Run ChIP-seq quality control with input.bam and chip.bam using the chipseq_qc workflow.
output_examples.output: The skill generates a bash workflow script via workflow_generator.py that runs multiBamSummary, plotCorrelation, plotPCA, plotCoverage, bamPEFragmentSize, and plotFingerprint. Results include a correlation heatmap PNG, PCA plot, coverage assessment, fragment size histogram, and fingerprint plot with enrichment metrics.

output_examples.input: Create a heatmap of ChIP-seq signal around TSS regions using signal.bw and genes.bed, showing 3kb upstream and downstream.
output_examples.output: The skill generates the computeMatrix command to produce a compressed matrix file, then renders a heatmap with k-means clustering. The output includes a publication-quality heatmap PNG with genes sorted by signal intensity and a profile plot showing the average signal distribution around TSS.

best_practices: Always validate input BAM and BED files with validate_files.py before running analysis workflows to catch missing indexes and format errors early
Use consistent normalization methods across all samples being compared, and include effective genome size for RPGC normalization
Start with quality control steps like fingerprint plots and correlation analysis before proceeding to visualization and comparison
anti_patterns: Using raw count normalization when comparing samples with different sequencing depths - always use CPM, RPGC, or RPKM for cross-sample comparison
Extending reads in RNA-seq analysis using --extendReads which creates artifacts across splice junctions - RNA-seq should never extend reads
Running genome-wide analysis without first testing parameters on a small genomic region using --region chr1:1-1000000 to verify output quality

faq.question: What file formats does this skill support?
faq.answer: This skill works with BAM, bigWig, and BED files commonly used in NGS sequencing analysis.

faq.question: Do I need to install deepTools separately?
faq.answer: Yes, deepTools must be installed in your Python environment before using these workflows.

faq.question: What normalization should I use for ChIP-seq data?
faq.answer: Use RPGC normalization with the effective genome size for genome-wide coverage tracks in ChIP-seq.

faq.question: Can this skill analyze data from any sequencing method?
faq.answer: It supports ChIP-seq, RNA-seq, ATAC-seq, and other common NGS techniques with specific workflows.

faq.question: What is the difference between CPM and RPKM normalization?
faq.answer: CPM normalizes by total reads per million mapped reads, while RPKM additionally accounts for gene length.

faq.question: How do I handle very large BAM files?
faq.answer: Use the --region parameter to test on a small genomic region before full genome processing.
